summary: Family-local loading-indicator family with a core Spinner atom, adapter-owned context wrappers, and button-loading integration built on the same atom contract.
philosophy: Keep Spinner as a small loading atom that stays decorative by default and only becomes an announced status surface when product code opts in with ariaLabel.
Keep the seven synced visual styles and size scale source-owned in core so React and Vue render the same spinner geometry and metadata.
Keep ButtonSpinner and EmptyStateSpinner thin adapter-owned context wrappers rather than turning the core atom into a matrix of loading situations.
Keep spinner semantics family-local and honest: the atom emits stable local metadata today, but Spinner is not part of the wave-1 central semantic registry.

useWhen: The UI needs an indeterminate loading indicator for inline button loading, centered empty states, or standalone async feedback.
The product flow already matches ButtonSpinner or EmptyStateSpinner and benefits from the wrapper defaults for size, visual treatment, and local metadata.
The team wants React and Vue spinner behavior to stay aligned through one core SVG recipe, one preset CSS contract, and one shared adapter contract file.
avoidWhen: The experience needs determinate progress, percentage tracking, or skeleton structure rather than an indeterminate spinner.
The implementation cannot provide nearby loading text or a truthful ariaLabel when the spinner must announce status on its own.
The team expects the exploratory TinoSpinner animation assets or broader semantic brand/loading taxonomies from Figma to be part of the shipped API today.

automationCovers: Core recipe tests for default decorative behavior, seven synced variants, size mapping, and standalone status mode.
Preset CSS contract tests for light and dark indicator colors plus rotation animation wiring.
Shared spinner contract coverage across React and Vue, local context-wrapper tests, and Storybook introduction/taxonomy tests in both apps.
manualReviewRequired: Product-level decisions about when loading is decorative versus when a spinner should expose a standalone accessible status name.
Real browser and assistive-technology validation for longer loading sequences, repeated status announcements, and button-loading feel.
Reduced-motion quality and whether spinner-only loading states provide enough surrounding context in real product layouts.
notes: Spinner is still queued in Wave 2 of the audit plan and does not yet have a dedicated family audit doc.
The family already has strong core, preset, adapter, contract, and Storybook coverage despite the missing dedicated audit write-up.

openQuestions: Should Spinner eventually gain a clearer documented policy for when product teams should pair it with surrounding loading text versus using ariaLabel directly?
Should the exploratory TinoSpinner size and animation assets remain design-only references, or should any of that surface become a future shipped spinner API?
